purpose: Person-specific support JSON built from selected uploaded life-impact JSON entries plus custom additions for requested conditions, devices, identity details, trauma history, and caregiving context.
medical_safety_note: This JSON is for accessibility, self-care, support planning, and application logic. It is not a diagnosis, treatment plan, emergency plan, or substitute for medical or mental-health care. For urgent danger, severe allergic reaction, sudden leg swelling/pain, suicidal intent, or medical emergency, use local emergency or crisis services.
privacy_note: Contains sensitive health, trauma, gender, and life-history information. Store locally, avoid public repos, and only share with consent.

language_rules: Use they/them pronouns unless the person updates this preference.
Feminine titles/endearments listed here are allowed because the person specifically accepts them; do not generalize this into she/her or woman/female assumptions.
Use body-neutral, consent-based, non-shaming language for PMOS/PCOS, obesity, hygiene, sexual trauma, and medical care.
Avoid forced forgiveness, family-loyalty pressure, or “prove your disability” framing.

requested_items: borderline personality disorder: discouraged, petulant, self-destructive
gender dysphoria: nonbinary, they/them, feminine endearments
past homelessness: streets, couch/floor surfing, shelters

files.source_pdf: What Is Spoon Theory__ Eric K. Fanaee, MD_ Board Certified in Pain Medicine and Anesthesiology.pdf
A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

Types of Endocrine Disorders _ UI Health.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Manual Wheelchair
life_limitations: May require learning, fit/measurement, maintenance, storage, transportation space, accessible routes, and social confidence to use well.
Can be limited by stairs, narrow doorways, uneven ground, snow/ice, inaccessible bathrooms, crowded spaces, or stigma.
May require upper-body strength or another person for propulsion depending on chair/person; inaccessible spaces and transport can be major barriers.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can improve safety, independence, comfort, confidence, and participation by reducing falls, pain, fatigue, or distance barriers.
Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
Can make indoor/outdoor movement possible with reduced walking/standing demands and may support everyday activities despite reduced mobility.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Usually reduces mobility spoon cost when matched well; may add setup/transportation spoon cost.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.device_description: manual/power-driven device for indoor/outdoor locomotion when mobility is reduced; lightweight/less expensive/easy to maintain
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Your Complete Mobility Guide_ A Simple Guide to Mobility Aids _ Southern.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Mobility aid/device
life_limitations: May require learning, fit/measurement, maintenance, storage, transportation space, accessible routes, and social confidence to use well.
Can be limited by stairs, narrow doorways, uneven ground, snow/ice, inaccessible bathrooms, crowded spaces, or stigma.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can improve safety, independence, comfort, confidence, and participation by reducing falls, pain, fatigue, or distance barriers.
Can conserve spoons by making movement less physically expensive.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Usually reduces mobility spoon cost when matched well; may add setup/transportation spoon cost.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.device_description: device designed to help people with problems moving around complete daily activities and increase freedom/independence
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Your Complete Mobility Guide_ A Simple Guide to Mobility Aids _ Southern.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Use of Mobility Aids
life_limitations: Can limit the specific daily/work function named here: Use of Mobility Aids.
May make tasks slower, less safe, more painful, more tiring, or less reliable unless the environment is adjusted.
Can interact with other conditions, so a person may need supports that address multiple limitations at once.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Naming the limitation can make accommodation conversations more practical because support can focus on the actual barrier instead of only the diagnosis.
Assistive technology, altered timing, task redesign, or environmental changes can slightly improve independence and reduce exhaustion.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost rises when the task repeatedly demands this function without breaks or tools.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome
life_limitations: May limit stamina, pain tolerance, immune safety, digestion, breathing, sleep, temperature tolerance, medical scheduling, or physical reliability.
Can make work/school, errands, self-care, food planning, socializing, and travel harder due to symptoms, flares, treatment, or environmental triggers.
Invisible symptoms may be misunderstood, causing pressure to overexert.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Medical recognition and accommodations can support safer pacing, trigger reduction, treatment access, and clearer expectations.
Adaptive routines can slightly improve autonomy and reduce flares or crashes.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost often fluctuates with flares, treatment side effects, pain, fatigue, weather, food, sleep, and stress.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Back Impairment
life_limitations: May limit walking, standing, lifting, reaching, transfers, balance, speed, endurance, pain control, and access to inaccessible spaces.
Can make errands, bathing, cooking, transportation, cleaning, work sites, and social activities dependent on ramps, seating, rest, or assistance.
Fall risk, pain flares, and inaccessible buildings can reduce independence even when the person has strong coping skills.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Mobility aids, ergonomic tools, accessible layouts, and pacing can improve safety, range, and dignity.
Planning around energy, pain, and access can slightly increase participation and reduce injury.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Physical spoon cost rises with distance, stairs, carrying, transfers, and repeated movements.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

Fall risk, pain flares, and inaccessible buildings can reduce independence even when the person has strong coping skills.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Mobility aids, ergonomic tools, accessible layouts, and pacing can improve safety, range, and dignity.

selected_source_entries.name: Borderline Personality Disorder
life_limitations: Can affect emotion regulation, identity, trust, boundaries, attachment, impulse control, conflict, and relationship stability.
May lead to intense rejection sensitivity, withdrawal, anger, control struggles, dependency, isolation, or repeated interpersonal crises depending on the pattern.
Daily functioning can be limited by the energy spent managing feelings, perceived threat, shame, or social consequences.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Recognition can support skills-based treatment, clearer boundaries, repair conversations, and less self-blame.
Consistent routines, validation, and agreed coping steps can slightly improve relationship safety and self-control.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost spikes during conflict, abandonment fear, criticism, shame, or interpersonal uncertainty.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Alphabetical Listing of Mental Disorders.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Personality Disorder
life_limitations: May limit concentration, emotional regulation, reliability, sleep, stress tolerance, communication, social safety, or ability to handle conflict and change.
Can make work/school, appointments, self-care, relationships, and public settings harder even if the disability is invisible.
Stigma and fear of disclosure may create extra barriers to accommodations.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Disclosure to safe people or formal accommodation processes can open access to flexible schedules, quiet space, written instructions, and supportive check-ins.
Skills, treatment, routines, and validation can slightly improve consistency and reduce shame.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Mental load may drain spoons before visible tasks start, especially under stress or masking.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Reactive Attachment Disorder
selected_source_entries.source_group: mental health / psychiatric / neurological condition listed by source
life_limitations: May limit daily functioning through symptoms that affect mood, thinking, behavior, relationships, body comfort, sleep, communication, or safety.
Can create extra effort for tasks that other people may consider automatic, especially under stress or without accommodations.
Stigma and misunderstanding can add social and practical barriers beyond the symptoms themselves.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: A clear name can help the person seek information, support, accommodations, and more compassionate self-understanding.
Tracking patterns can slightly improve planning, communication, and access to help.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: professional care if distress or impairment is significant
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost depends on symptom severity, support, sleep, stress, comorbidities, and environment.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Alphabetical Listing of Mental Disorders.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Post Traumatic Stress Disorder
life_limitations: Can shrink the person’s world through avoidance, panic, hypervigilance, intrusive worry, and fear of places/situations where escape or safety feels uncertain.
May limit appointments, errands, work/school, social connection, sleep, concentration, travel, conflict resolution, and asking for help.
The person may appear “fine” while spending a large amount of internal energy monitoring threat or preventing a panic/trauma response.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Recognizing the condition can support grounding skills, exposure at a tolerable pace, trauma-informed care, and clearer boundaries.
Predictable routines, safety plans, and permission to pause can slightly improve autonomy and reduce shame.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Variable spoon cost; even short tasks may become expensive when they involve crowds, uncertainty, conflict, trauma cues, or sensory overload.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Alphabetical Listing of Mental Disorders.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
life_limitations: May limit concentration, emotional regulation, reliability, sleep, stress tolerance, communication, social safety, or ability to handle conflict and change.
Can make work/school, appointments, self-care, relationships, and public settings harder even if the disability is invisible.
Stigma and fear of disclosure may create extra barriers to accommodations.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Disclosure to safe people or formal accommodation processes can open access to flexible schedules, quiet space, written instructions, and supportive check-ins.
Skills, treatment, routines, and validation can slightly improve consistency and reduce shame.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Mental load may drain spoons before visible tasks start, especially under stress or masking.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Gender Dysphoria
life_limitations: Can affect intimacy, body safety, pain, consent communication, relationships, shame, medical care, or gender-related distress depending on the condition.
May make closeness, touch, dating, pelvic/sexual health care, or identity expression emotionally and physically complicated.
Stigma can prevent people from seeking support.
Can be intensified by misgendering, unsafe environments, barriers to gender-affirming care, or inability to express identity safely.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Recognition can support trauma-informed care, pelvic/sexual health treatment, safer communication, and identity-affirming support when relevant.
Clear consent and pacing can slightly improve safety, trust, and comfort.
Affirming names/pronouns, privacy, and safe identity expression can improve comfort and reduce distress.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost depends on pain, dysphoria, shame, fear, relational safety, and sensory needs.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Alphabetical Listing of Mental Disorders.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder
life_limitations: Can affect attention, communication, sensory processing, speech/language, learning, coordination, executive functioning, memory, transitions, and social expectations.
May make school/work, forms, appointments, chores, hygiene, crowded places, multitasking, or verbal instructions harder without accommodations.
Mismatch between the person’s needs and the environment can be more disabling than the diagnosis alone.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Understanding the profile can unlock accommodations, assistive technology, communication supports, routines, special interests, and strengths-based planning.
Clear structure and sensory-respectful environments can slightly improve independence, confidence, and task completion.
External structure, novelty, body-doubling, and reward systems can make tasks more doable and may harness creativity or quick pattern-shifting.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost often rises with transitions, sensory overload, unclear instructions, masking, or executive-function demands.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Alphabetical Listing of Mental Disorders.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Attention Deficit/Hyperactivity
life_limitations: May limit communication, executive functioning, sensory tolerance, reading/writing, attention, social navigation, or learning in standard environments.
Can make multitasking, vague instructions, loud spaces, transitions, or rapid deadlines disproportionately exhausting.
The person may be capable but needs a different format, pace, or sensory setup.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Accommodations can reveal strengths by removing unnecessary barriers in communication, timing, sensory load, or format.
Assistive technology, visual supports, and predictable routines can slightly improve independence and task completion.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost increases with sensory overload, transitions, masking, and executive-function demands.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder
life_limitations: Can affect attention, communication, sensory processing, speech/language, learning, coordination, executive functioning, memory, transitions, and social expectations.
May make school/work, forms, appointments, chores, hygiene, crowded places, multitasking, or verbal instructions harder without accommodations.
Mismatch between the person’s needs and the environment can be more disabling than the diagnosis alone.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Understanding the profile can unlock accommodations, assistive technology, communication supports, routines, special interests, and strengths-based planning.
Clear structure and sensory-respectful environments can slightly improve independence, confidence, and task completion.
Respecting sensory needs and communication style can let strengths such as deep focus, pattern recognition, honesty, or specialized interests show more easily.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Spoon cost often rises with transitions, sensory overload, unclear instructions, masking, or executive-function demands.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Alphabetical Listing of Mental Disorders.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Stress
life_limitations: Can become a barrier when disability-related symptoms interfere with the work function: Stress.
May limit employment access, safety, consistency, or productivity if the task/environment is not adapted.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Identifying the affected work function can guide targeted accommodations instead of vague expectations.
Redesigning the function, environment, timing, or tools can slightly improve participation and job retention.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Work-function demands can drain spoons repeatedly across a shift; supports should reduce repeated friction.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: A to Z of Disabilities and Accommodations.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Spoon Theory / limited daily energy
life_limitations: Can limit how many tasks, appointments, chores, social plans, and care activities fit into a day.
Forces tradeoffs: doing one task may mean losing capacity for another, even when the person wants to do both.
Can make invisible effort hard for others to understand.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Provides a simple shared language for boundaries, pacing, task prioritization, and compassion.
Can slightly improve planning by making energy visible and countable.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Every task should have an estimated spoon cost and a recovery option.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.concept_description: A person has a limited amount of physical and mental energy to spend on daily tasks.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: What Is Spoon Theory__ Eric K. Fanaee, MD_ Board Certified in Pain Medicine and Anesthesiology.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Day-to-day variability
life_limitations: Makes future planning difficult because tomorrow’s capacity may not match today’s.
Can cause guilt or conflict when commitments must change.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Encourages flexible planning, backup options, and permission to reschedule without shame.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Use ranges rather than fixed costs; include flare/crash mode.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.concept_description: Spoon cost for the same task can vary by day.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: What Is Spoon Theory__ Eric K. Fanaee, MD_ Board Certified in Pain Medicine and Anesthesiology.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Invisible illness visibility
life_limitations: Can cause disbelief, pressure to overperform, lack of accommodations, and social misunderstanding.
May make people feel obligated to explain private health details.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: A shared term such as “spoonie” can help people find community and explain needs quickly if they choose.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: avoid judging how energy is spent
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Social invalidation itself costs spoons.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.concept_description: Many chronic conditions are not immediately visible.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: What Is Spoon Theory__ Eric K. Fanaee, MD_ Board Certified in Pain Medicine and Anesthesiology.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Boundary communication
life_limitations: People may still judge how someone spends limited energy or expect justification.
The person may need to protect energy by saying no.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Can make it easier to ask for help, explain cancellations, and honor needs without over-explaining.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Boundary-setting may cost spoons at first but can prevent larger crashes later.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.concept_description: Spoon language can communicate limits without a full medical explanation.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: What Is Spoon Theory__ Eric K. Fanaee, MD_ Board Certified in Pain Medicine and Anesthesiology.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Community and peer support
life_limitations: Without community, chronic illness/disability can feel isolating and misunderstood.
Peer spaces can still be tiring if comparison or unsolicited advice becomes overwhelming.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Peer support can offer emotional support, practical tips, identity validation, and reduced isolation.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Community can return spoons emotionally, but only when it is safe and not demanding.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.concept_description: The spoonie community can offer shared experience and support.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: What Is Spoon Theory__ Eric K. Fanaee, MD_ Board Certified in Pain Medicine and Anesthesiology.pdf

selected_source_entries.name: Physical abuse
life_limitations: Can involve unsafe relationships, neglect, abuse, or relational distress that affects housing, trust, emotional regulation, safety, sleep, and daily stability.
May reduce autonomy if the person depends on unsafe people or lacks support.
Trauma and practical consequences can interfere with work/school, care, and self-protection.
possible_life_improvements_or_supports: Naming the issue can support safety planning, advocacy, protective services, therapy, and healthier boundaries.
Stable support and practical resources can slightly improve safety and decision-making.
selected_source_entries.spoon_energy_notes: High spoon cost when safety is uncertain; stabilization may need to come before productivity.
selected_source_entries.individual_variability_note: Impact can be mild, moderate, severe, intermittent, progressive, or invisible depending on the person and context.
selected_source_entries.source_pdf: Alphabetical Listing of Mental Disorders.pdf

freshness_or_external_notes.note: The uploaded JSONs did not include a PCOS/PMOS entry. Current public medical sources in May 2026 reported that PCOS was renamed Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome (PMOS). This JSON retains PCOS as an alias for older records and search compatibility.

person_specific_entries.label: Shower chair
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: A bathroom safety aid that can reduce standing demand, fall risk, pain flares, dizziness, fatigue, and post-shower crashes.
person_specific_life_limitations: Showers may require standing, balance, reaching, temperature tolerance, sensory tolerance, and recovery energy all at once.
Without seating, bathing can become unsafe or too exhausting, especially with pain, joint instability, ankle injury, scoliosis, varicose vein discomfort, MCAS triggers, or fatigue.
Bathroom tasks may carry shame or urgency if the person feels they “should” be able to do them without help.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: A stable chair/bench, grab bars, handheld shower head, non-slip mat, reachable supplies, and pre/post rest can make bathing safer and less spoon-expensive.
Turning showering into tiny steps can make hygiene possible on low-capacity days.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: stable shower chair or transfer bench
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Can reduce shower spoon cost, but setup, drying, dressing, and recovery still count.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Offer “tiny shower” options: face/underarms, rinse only, seated full shower, hair wash as separate task.
Reward partial completion; hygiene support should never be all-or-nothing.

person_specific_entries.label: Manual wheelchair
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: A mobility aid that can conserve energy, reduce pain from standing/walking, expand distance tolerance, and support safer participation when accessible routes exist.
person_specific_life_limitations: Use can be limited by stairs, narrow doors, inaccessible bathrooms, transportation barriers, uneven surfaces, weather, upper-body fatigue, pain, stigma, and needing help with transfers or loading.
Manual propulsion may be difficult during pain, fatigue, joint instability, shoulder/wrist strain, MCAS flare, ADHD overwhelm, or anxiety in crowds.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Accessible routes, seating-height planning, wheelchair-friendly layouts, push assistance when consented to, and rest breaks can increase independence and reduce overexertion.
Using the wheelchair proactively can prevent crashes rather than only after the person is already depleted.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Often lowers mobility cost but may add transfer, route-planning, loading, propulsion, and social-stigma costs.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Do not frame wheelchair use as failure; frame it as energy conservation, pain prevention, and access.
Tasks should include wheelchair-friendly steps and alternatives.

person_specific_entries.label: Mast Cell Activation Syndrome (MCAS)
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: A trigger-reactive condition where foods, scents, heat, stress, exertion, medications, fabrics, chemicals, or environmental exposures may cause flares. The uploaded JSONs did not include an exact MCAS entry, so allergy/sensitivity entries were used as related source material.
person_specific_life_limitations: Food, scent, heat, exertion, stress, and chemical exposure can make daily life unpredictable.
Can limit shopping, cooking, cleaning, travel, appointments, social contact, bathing, and shared spaces when triggers are hard to control.
Fear of reactions can increase anxiety and avoidance, especially after repeated dismissal.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Trigger tracking, low-scent environments, safe-food lists, low-exertion routines, and clinician-guided emergency plans can reduce preventable flares.
Having trusted people believe symptoms can reduce stress-trigger cycles.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: medication and emergency plan with clinician
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Flares may rapidly reduce available spoons; prevention and recovery tasks should be rewarded as real care.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Store trigger lists as editable data, not fixed assumptions.
Use “possible trigger” language, because triggers can change.

person_specific_entries.label: Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome (PMOS), formerly PCOS
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: The uploaded JSONs did not include a PCOS/PMOS-specific entry. This profile keeps both PMOS and PCOS aliases for search compatibility and links the person-specific impact to endocrine/metabolic strain, insulin resistance risk, cycle/hormone symptoms, pain/fatigue, body stigma, and gender dysphoria considerations.
person_specific_life_limitations: Hormone and metabolic symptoms can affect energy, appetite, weight, pain, skin/hair changes, sleep, mood, bleeding/cycle unpredictability, and long-term metabolic risk.
Medical settings may increase dysphoria when staff assume womanhood or use gendered reproductive language.
Weight stigma and dismissal can make care harder to access.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Body-neutral, gender-affirming care; glucose/insulin-risk monitoring as directed by clinicians; symptom tracking; and low-shame food planning can support stability.
Keeping PCOS as an alias helps older paperwork, doctors, insurance, and search systems while PMOS terminology adoption spreads.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: keep PMOS and PCOS aliases in search fields
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Hormone shifts, pain, fatigue, and food planning can change daily capacity; avoid treating consistency as a character test.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Do not use “women-only” wording; use “person with PMOS/PCOS” unless the person chooses otherwise.
Reward glucose-supportive meals, hydration, medication routines, and appointments without moralizing food.

person_specific_entries.label: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome, hypermobile type / hEDS
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Connective tissue instability can affect joints, pain, fatigue, posture, walking, standing, transfers, fine motor tasks, digestion/autonomic symptoms for some people, and recovery after exertion.
person_specific_life_limitations: May limit walking, standing, stairs, bathing, chores, lifting, sleep, grip, reaching, and repetitive tasks due to pain, subluxation/instability, fatigue, and injury risk.
Can make “simple” tasks cost a lot more energy because the body works harder to stabilize itself.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Joint protection, pacing, adaptive equipment, safe strengthening/physical therapy guidance, ergonomic setups, and mobility aids can improve safety and reduce crashes.
Validating pain and instability helps prevent overexertion.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Standing and transitions can cost more than the visible task. Recovery time should be included.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Break physical tasks into tiny body-safe steps.
Offer “sit-down version” and “do from bed/chair version” for chores and self-care.

person_specific_entries.label: Pre-diabetes
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: A metabolic risk state where blood sugar stability, food access, stress, sleep, PMOS/PCOS, body weight, pain, and activity limitations can interact.
person_specific_life_limitations: Meal planning can be harder with low spoons, poverty/food insecurity history, executive dysfunction, sensory needs, MCAS food limits, and trauma around being judged.
Pain and mobility limits can make movement-based advice inaccessible or unsafe if not adapted.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Low-shame, realistic food routines; hydration; clinician follow-up; and accessible movement or seated activity when safe can support stability.
Stable food access and predictable routines matter more than perfection.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Blood sugar swings and meal decisions can drain cognitive and physical energy.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Reward eating enough, hydrating, checking in, and choosing a realistic option; do not punish cravings or missed routines.

person_specific_entries.label: Scoliosis
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: A spine/posture condition that can contribute to back pain, uneven fatigue, standing/walking strain, seating needs, and difficulty with lifting, reaching, bathing, chores, and sleep positioning.
person_specific_life_limitations: May limit standing tolerance, walking distance, bending, lifting, showering, transfers, sleep comfort, and prolonged sitting.
Can interact with hEDS, ankle injury, obesity, wheelchair seating, and varicose vein discomfort.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Supportive seating, posture breaks, adapted chores, physical therapy guidance when available, and pain-aware pacing can reduce barriers.
A wheelchair cushion/positioning check may reduce secondary pain.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Posture maintenance itself can cost energy; include breaks before pain escalates.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Offer alternatives to bending/lifting tasks.
Allow “move position” and “lie down” breaks as task steps.

person_specific_entries.label: Ankle injury
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: An ankle injury can reduce safe weight-bearing, balance, stairs, transfers, shower safety, errands, standing chores, and confidence walking.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can increase fall risk, pain, swelling, uneven gait, and overuse of other joints.
May worsen when the person pushes through because a task feels urgent or guilt-driven.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Rest, elevation/compression if medically appropriate, mobility aid use, seated tasks, safe footwear, accessible routes, and medical follow-up for worsening symptoms can help.
Planning tasks around seated options can prevent reinjury.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Pain, swelling, and guarding can increase energy cost even if the person completes the task.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Do not require step counts or walking-based rewards.
Allow wheelchair/seated task completion to count fully.

person_specific_entries.label: Varicose veins
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Varicose veins can contribute to leg aching, heaviness, swelling, skin sensitivity, heat discomfort, and reduced standing tolerance.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make standing chores, showering, waiting in lines, heat exposure, and long sitting/standing more uncomfortable.
May interact with ankle injury, wheelchair seating, obesity, pain, and fatigue.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Leg elevation, movement breaks, avoiding long static standing, temperature management, and clinician-guided compression or treatment options can help some people.
Accessible seating in every room can reduce symptom escalation.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: ask clinician about compression if appropriate
watch for urgent symptoms like sudden swelling/pain
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Leg discomfort can turn short tasks into high-cost tasks; breaks should be expected.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Standing tasks should have seated alternatives and short timers.
Do not frame rest/elevation as laziness.

person_specific_entries.label: Morbid obesity / severe obesity
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Higher body weight can affect mobility, pain, endurance, seating/transfer needs, blood sugar risk, heat tolerance, medical access, and social stigma. The support profile should treat body size as an access need, not a moral failure.
person_specific_life_limitations: May limit access to chairs, bathrooms, mobility equipment, clothing, medical equipment, safe movement options, and respectful care.
Weight stigma can worsen avoidance of appointments, eating shame, and emotional distress.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Bariatric-capacity equipment when needed, body-neutral care, accessible seating, low-shame nourishment, and pain-safe activity options can improve safety and dignity.
Support works best when it removes barriers instead of shaming the body.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Pain, stigma, heat, seating barriers, and medical anxiety can all add hidden energy costs.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Use “care for the body you have today” framing.
Reward nourishment, hydration, rest, and appointments; never shame size.

person_specific_entries.label: Borderline Personality Disorder — discouraged, petulant, self-destructive patterns
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Emotion regulation, abandonment fear, relationship insecurity, intense shame, anger, internalized distress, impulsive or self-destructive urges, and people-pleasing may all show up differently depending on the discouraged, petulant, or self-destructive pattern active that day.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make conflict, perceived rejection, unclear tone, delays in replies, caregiver stress, or sudden changes feel unsafe or overwhelming.

Discouraged/quiet patterns may hide crisis behind compliance; petulant patterns may come out as protest, anger, control-seeking, or intense dissatisfaction; self-destructive patterns may involve self-hatred or urges to give up on care.
Shame can make the person avoid asking for help until distress is severe.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: DBT-style validation, predictable reassurance, clear boundaries, repair after conflict, crisis plans, and tiny next steps can reduce spirals.
Feeling believed and not abandoned can improve willingness to use coping skills.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Emotion storms can consume spoons like physical flares; recovery after conflict counts as care.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Use “I believe this feels huge” before offering steps.
Never punish emotional intensity; redirect to safety, grounding, and one doable next action.
Support bot should ask: comfort, practical help, distraction, or problem-solving?

person_specific_entries.label: Reactive Attachment Disorder / attachment injury
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Early attachment disruption can affect trust, closeness, help-seeking, fear of rejection, emotional safety, and the ability to believe support will stay.
person_specific_life_limitations: May cause shutdown, clinginess, distrust, testing, fear of being too much, or difficulty accepting care.
Support can feel unsafe even when wanted, especially after abandonment, foster care, sibling separation, abuse, or repeated housing loss.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Consistency, clear communication, consent, follow-through, gentle repair, and predictable routines can make support feel safer.
Care works best when it is reliable without being controlling.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Relational uncertainty can drain energy even when no physical task is happening.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Use stable, warm language and explain changes clearly.
When plans change, include reassurance and the next concrete step.

person_specific_entries.label: C-PTSD / complex trauma
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Complex trauma can affect nervous system regulation, trust, memory, sleep, body safety, self-worth, relationships, executive function, and responses to authority, conflict, or unpredictability.
person_specific_life_limitations: May cause hypervigilance, shutdown, flashbacks, nightmares, dissociation, avoidance, anger, shame, or difficulty asking for help.
Multiple trauma layers can make ordinary tasks feel threatening if they echo past control, abandonment, punishment, or violation.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Grounding, trauma-informed choice, consent, predictable routines, safe exits, body-neutral language, and therapist/clinical support can improve stability.
Reducing threat cues often helps more than increasing pressure.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Trauma activation can create physical exhaustion; recovery time should be treated as necessary.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Do not force trauma retelling.
Use gentle opt-in prompts and give choices.
During overwhelm, offer grounding before tasks.

person_specific_entries.label: Loss of major support systems
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Losing major supports can remove practical help, emotional regulation, transportation, safety, housing stability, financial backup, and a sense of belonging.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can intensify abandonment fear, overwhelm, grief, isolation, executive dysfunction, and crisis risk.
Tasks that used to be shared may become impossible or much more expensive.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Rebuilding a support map, identifying safe contacts, creating backup plans, and using low-pressure community supports can reduce isolation.
Reliable micro-supports can matter as much as one big support system.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Grief and isolation consume energy; social recovery tasks count as care.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Include “who can help?” fields without assuming family is safe.
Offer scripts for asking for help in tiny ways.

person_specific_entries.label: Separation of siblings
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Sibling separation can create grief, attachment injury, identity disruption, guilt, anger, and long-term sensitivity to being replaced, forgotten, or excluded.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can amplify abandonment fears, family-triggered grief, distrust, and distress around holidays, milestones, adoption stories, or sibling favoritism.
May make chosen-family stability especially important.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Validating grief, allowing mixed feelings, preserving safe memories, and supporting chosen family can reduce shame and isolation.
Contact or reconnection should be consent-based and safety-aware.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Family grief can unexpectedly drain capacity; allow low-demand days around triggers.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Do not minimize sibling loss as “just the past.”
Offer grounding around family-trigger dates.

person_specific_entries.label: Foster care history
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Foster care history can involve placement instability, loss of belongings, attachment disruption, adult betrayal, survival compliance, and fear that care is conditional.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can create difficulty trusting systems, fear of being sent away, people-pleasing, guardedness, or intense distress when housing/relationships feel unstable.
Rules, paperwork, appointments, or authority figures may trigger survival responses.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Predictable routines, clear rights, consent, transparent expectations, and stable belongings/spaces can support safety.
Reassurance works best when paired with consistent behavior.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: System-related tasks can cost extra energy because of trauma associations.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Use “you belong here” and “care is not earned by perfection” style scripts when appropriate.
Avoid surprise rule changes.

person_specific_entries.label: Sexual assault history
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Sexual assault history can affect body safety, medical care, bathing, touch, intimacy, sleep, dissociation, clothing, gender dysphoria, and trust.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can cause flashbacks, avoidance, hypervigilance, dissociation, body shame, or distress during medical exams, hygiene, intimacy, or unexpected touch.
Loss of control can make well-intended help feel threatening if consent is not explicit.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Consent-first support, choice, privacy, grounding, safe exits, trauma-informed medical care, and never forcing details can improve safety.
Control over timing, clothing, touch, and language matters.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Body-care tasks can be trauma tasks; partial hygiene and recovery count.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Hygiene reminders should be gentle and choice-based.
Never require details of the assault; support present-day safety instead.

person_specific_entries.label: Gender dysphoria — nonbinary, they/them, feminine titles/endearments welcomed
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: The person is nonbinary and uses they/them pronouns while also welcoming selected feminine titles or endearments such as “wife,” “,” and “baby girl.” Support should affirm both facts without treating them as contradictory.
person_specific_life_limitations: Misgendering, body-focused medical language, reproductive-health assumptions, paperwork, clothing/body changes, PMOS symptoms, and sexual trauma can all worsen dysphoria.
People may incorrectly assume feminine terms mean the person is a woman or uses she/her.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Consistent they/them pronouns, consent-based endearments, body-neutral wording, and gender-affirming medical notes can reduce distress.
Asking “what words feel good today?” can help because dysphoria may vary.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: allowed endearments: wife and baby girl
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Misgendering and dysphoria can drain energy and increase avoidance of care tasks.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Store pronouns and allowed endearments separately.
Use “person with PMOS” rather than gendered reproductive wording unless quoting a source.
avoid: using gendered body language without consent

person_specific_entries.label: Past homelessness — streets, couch/floor surfing, shelters
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Past homelessness can leave long-term survival stress around safety, possessions, sleep, food, privacy, paperwork, and fear of losing housing again.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make clutter, landlord issues, money stress, packed bags, shelters, couch/floor sleeping memories, or home instability highly triggering.
May increase hoarding-like survival saving, difficulty resting, sleep hypervigilance, and panic when routines change.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Housing stability cues, backup plans, document storage, pantry/supply predictability, and gentle decluttering without forced disposal can improve safety.
The home should feel like a protected recovery space, not a performance test.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Housing fear can consume background energy even on stable days.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Avoid “just throw it away” language; ask what items mean for safety.
Reward home-stability tasks like paperwork, supplies, and rest.

person_specific_entries.label: Repeated loss of homes
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Repeated loss of homes can create grief, instability sensitivity, difficulty feeling settled, fear of attachment to places/things, and intense distress around moves or conflict in the home.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make home-related tasks emotionally loaded: cleaning, organizing, rent, repairs, packing, or decorating may trigger fear or grief.
The person may need reassurance that care, housing, and belonging are not conditional on productivity.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Stable routines, safe-home rituals, backup planning, and gentle organization can rebuild a sense of control.
Small home comforts can be therapeutic, not frivolous.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Home-loss grief can make chores feel like trauma work.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Frame home tasks as “making the nest safer,” not proving worth.
Offer low-demand home care options.

person_specific_entries.label: Abusive mother
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Abuse by a mother can complicate attachment, self-worth, boundaries, guilt, caregiving roles, family contact, and the meaning of “” as either a chosen/gentle term or a trigger depending on context.
person_specific_life_limitations: May create intense reactions to criticism, control, yelling, silent treatment, comparison, or conditional affection.
Can fuel people-pleasing, shame, fear of being bad, or fear of becoming abusive.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Boundaries, chosen-family validation, no-forced-forgiveness support, and safe scripts can reduce harm.
Affectionate titles should be used only in the person’s affirmed, chosen way.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Family contact or memories can drain capacity before and after the event.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Avoid “but she is your mother” framing.
Use gentle chosen-family language when requested.

person_specific_entries.label: Abusive adoptive parents
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Abuse by adoptive parents can compound attachment trauma, belonging insecurity, identity grief, distrust of caregivers, and fear that acceptance can be withdrawn.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make adoption-related language, family expectations, gratitude pressure, and authority figures triggering.
May worsen fears of replacement, rejection, and not being “really” family.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Validation without gratitude pressure, consent-based family contact, and affirming chosen belonging can improve emotional safety.
Support should recognize both adoption loss and abuse without forcing a simple narrative.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Adoption/family triggers may require recovery time.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Avoid “they saved you” narratives.
Let the person define family and belonging.

person_specific_entries.label: Sibling favoritism after adoption
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Sibling favoritism after adoption can intensify feelings of replaceability, not being chosen, conditional worth, jealousy, grief, and shame.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make comparisons, unequal attention, family stories, gifts, holidays, or sibling milestones painful.
May feed imposter syndrome, people-pleasing, anger, withdrawal, or fear of asking for needs.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Explicit reassurance, fairness, naming the wound without shame, and avoiding comparisons can reduce triggers.
Chosen-family rituals can help replace old comparison patterns.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Comparison triggers can cause emotional crashes; allow regulation before tasks.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Avoid comparing progress to anyone else.
Use personalized goals and rewards.

person_specific_entries.label: Caregiver for disabled partner
person_specific_entries.category: caregiving role / mutual disability / responsibility load
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: The person provides care while also living with their own disabilities, pain, trauma, executive dysfunction, and energy limits. Support must protect both the partner’s needs and the caregiver’s body/mind.
person_specific_life_limitations: Care tasks can conflict with pain, fatigue, mobility limits, sensory/MCAS triggers, ADHD/autism overwhelm, and trauma responses.
Guilt may push the person to overextend, people-please, or hide their own needs until burnout.
If the disabled partner also relies heavily on them, rest may feel like abandonment even when it is necessary.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Tiny-step caregiving tasks, shared planning, respite, backup supports, rewards for self-care, and explicit permission to rest can reduce burnout.
A fair system should reward care work and self-care equally.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Caregiving uses emotional, executive, physical, and sensory spoons; recovery must be built in.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Caregiver tasks should be split into micro-steps and reward partial progress.
The system should never require the caregiver to sacrifice food, sleep, medication, hygiene, or pain management to earn care credit.

person_specific_entries.label: Severe ADHD
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Severe ADHD can affect initiation, memory, time awareness, transitions, emotional regulation, prioritizing, organizing, follow-through, sleep rhythm, and task completion.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make multi-step tasks feel impossible without external structure.
Urgency, shame, or overwhelm can lead to avoidance, paralysis, impulsive switching, or burnout.
Medical and care routines may be missed even when the person cares deeply.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Timers, visual checklists, body doubling, reminders, gamified rewards, fewer choices, and tiny “start here” actions can improve follow-through.
Rewarding task initiation and partial progress is essential.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Executive-function cost is real; planning and starting tasks should count as work.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Every task should have a tiny first step and a “resume where you left off” option.
Do not erase progress if interrupted.

person_specific_entries.label: Autism Spectrum Disorder
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Autism can affect sensory processing, transitions, communication, social energy, routines, shutdown/meltdown risk, food/sensory tolerance, special interests, and need for predictability.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can make noise, scents, textures, lights, social demands, uncertainty, and transitions exhausting or painful.
Misread tone or unclear expectations may cause anxiety or conflict.
Food and hygiene tasks may be affected by sensory barriers, not defiance.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Predictable routines, sensory-friendly settings, clear literal communication, transition warnings, safe foods, low-demand recovery, and respect for special interests can help.
Choice and consent reduce overwhelm.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Sensory load can drain energy before tasks begin; recovery from overload counts.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Offer visual, concrete options rather than vague prompts.
Include sensory reasons in task alternatives.

person_specific_entries.label: Anxiety and stress
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Anxiety and stress can affect body symptoms, sleep, appetite, attention, decision-making, social contact, medical care, conflict tolerance, and ability to start tasks.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can cause avoidance, panic, irritability, reassurance seeking, shutdown, racing thoughts, or physical symptoms.
Stress can worsen pain, MCAS flares, blood sugar stability, BPD symptoms, trauma responses, and executive dysfunction.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Grounding, predictable plans, reduced demands, body-based regulation, support scripts, and breaking decisions into small choices can improve function.
Validation before advice helps reduce threat response.
support_or_accommodation_ideas: breathing or sensory regulation if tolerated
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Anxiety uses physical and cognitive energy; rest after panic/stress is valid.
app_or_support_bot_notes: When stress is high, switch from productivity mode to regulation mode.
Offer comfort/practical/distraction/problem-solving choices.

person_specific_entries.label: Imposter syndrome
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: Imposter syndrome can make the person doubt their disability, pain, need for help, caregiving value, identity, or right to accommodations even when evidence supports those needs.
person_specific_life_limitations: May lead to overexplaining, overworking, refusing help, minimizing symptoms, or feeling fraudulent for using aids or rest.
Can be intensified by abuse, adoption favoritism, medical dismissal, and invisible illness.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Evidence logs, validation, accommodations without proof battles, and strengths-based reflections can reduce self-doubt.
Support should separate worth from productivity.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Self-doubt adds cognitive load and can delay care until crisis.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Use “needing help is data, not failure.”
Track wins and completed care without comparison.

person_specific_entries.label: People-pleasing
person_specific_entries.summary_for_this_person: People-pleasing may function as a safety strategy after abuse, abandonment, foster care, adoption trauma, sibling favoritism, sexual assault, and unstable housing. It may look like saying yes while internally overwhelmed.
person_specific_life_limitations: Can cause overcommitment, resentment, burnout, delayed needs, hidden distress, and difficulty saying no to caregiving or family demands.
The person may need permission and scripts to set boundaries without feeling cruel or unsafe.
what_can_slightly_improve_life: Boundary scripts, consent-based requests, “no” practice, rest permissions, and reassurance that love is not earned through self-erasure can help.
Support should praise honest limits, not just helpfulness.
person_specific_entries.spoon_energy_notes: Saying yes when depleted creates debt; boundary-setting protects future spoons.
app_or_support_bot_notes: Include buttons like “I need help,” “I need a pause,” “I can do part of this,” and “not today.”
Reward setting a healthy boundary.
avoid: praising self-sacrifice as the only good caregiving

combined_impact_patterns.label: Mobility, pain, and access stack
combined_impact_patterns.impact: Standing, walking, stairs, showering, transfers, errands, chores, appointments, and social plans may require seating, pacing, accessible routes, and recovery. Wheelchair/shower-chair use should be normalized as prevention and access, not failure.
support_design: keep mobility aid use visible as valid completion

combined_impact_patterns.label: Immune/metabolic/body regulation stack
combined_impact_patterns.impact: Food, scents, heat, exertion, stress, hormones, glucose stability, and medical stigma can interact. Support must avoid shame and preserve flexible safe-food choices.

combined_impact_patterns.label: Trauma, attachment, family, and housing loss stack
combined_impact_patterns.impact: Uncertainty, criticism, comparison, abandonment, family pressure, housing instability, medical touch, and loss of control may trigger panic, shutdown, anger, fawning, shame, or self-destructive urges.

combined_impact_patterns.label: ADHD/autism/anxiety executive-function stack
combined_impact_patterns.impact: Task initiation, transitions, sensory load, decision-making, shame, time blindness, social demands, and unclear instructions may be the actual barrier, not motivation.

combined_impact_patterns.label: Disabled caregiver role strain
combined_impact_patterns.impact: Caregiving while disabled can create emotional, physical, executive, and sensory overuse. The system should protect the caregiver from earning rewards only by self-sacrifice.
support_design: rest as a scheduled care task

task_design_defaults: Every task should have a tiny-step version, seated version, low-sensory version, and pause/resume option.
Partial completion should earn credit, especially for hygiene, eating, hydration, DBT skills, caregiver work, and home-stability tasks.
Do not reset progress after interruptions; interruptions are expected with disability, ADHD, trauma, and caregiving.
Ask whether the person wants comfort, practical help, distraction, or problem-solving before giving advice.
Use trauma-informed, non-shaming language and never require disclosure of trauma details.
reward_priorities_high_value: medication or medical routine if applicable
decompression after conflict or sensory overload

do_not_do: Do not treat mobility aids as giving up.
Do not shame food, body size, hygiene struggles, missed tasks, emotional intensity, or rest.
Do not assume family is safe or that forgiveness/contact is required.
Do not force details about sexual assault, abuse, foster care, homelessness, or family trauma.
Do not assume she/her or womanhood because of PMOS/PCOS or feminine endearments.
Do not motivate through abandonment threats, guilt, comparison, punishment, or loss of care.
